text: Unit III Activity Based Scheduling. Introduction: Activity Based Scheduling is a project scheduling approach that focuses on identifying, organizing and sequencing all individuals activities required to complete project. Divides the project into a list of well-defined activities. It provides a detailed view of what needs to be done, when, and what order, ensuring efficient resource utilization and timely project completion.

A complete project plan must include a timetable detailing the start and finish timeframes for each task. We will be able to do the following as a result: i) Ensure that the necessary resources are available at the precise moment needed. ii) Prevent many activities for the same resource at same time. iii) Create complete list of events who is performing each operation. iv) Develop details plan to measure actual performance. v) Make a cash flow prediction for a specific period of time.

Objectives of Planning: The objective of software project planning is to provide a framework that enables the manager to make reasonable estimates of resources, cost and schedule. i) Feasibility Assessment: A feasibility study is an assessment of feasibility of a proposed plan or project. It is not until we have constructed a detailed plan that we can forecast a completion date with any reasonable knowledge of its achievability.

ii) Resource Allocation: Resource Allocation is the process of allocating and scheduling available resources as efficiently and economically as possible. Project always need resources but these resources are usually sacrify. iii) Detailed Costing: After generating an activity plan and allocating particular resources, we will acquire extra complete estimates of prices and their timing. iv) Motivation: Providing targets and being seen to monitor achievements against target is an effective way of motivating staff particularly where they have been involve in setting those targets in the first place.

v) Co-ordination: The project plan offers a useful service means of communication and collaboration between team, especially for large projects with multiple project teams. Can be used as needed, will not be affected by forced shutdown. vi) Risk Management: By Breaking down the project into activities potential risks can be identified and managed at a more granular level. vii) Time Management: Activity planning helps in estimating the time required for each task and activity. viii) Decision Making: Activity planning provides a structured framework for making decisions throughout project lifecycle.

Project Schedules: A schedule in your project time table actually consists of sequenced of activities and milestones that are needed to be delivered under a given period of time. Project Schedule simply means a mechanism that is used to communicate and know about that tasks are needed and has to be done or performed and which organizational resources will be given or allocated tasks and what time durations or time frame work is needed to be performed.

The 8 steps of project scheduling process: Plan Schedule Management, Define the project activities, Determine dependencies, Sequence Activities, Estimate Resources, Estimate Durations, Develop project schedule, Monitor and control. 4 phases to developing a project schedule: i) The initial stage in creating a plans is deciding which activities must be completed and in what sequence they must be completed. ii) The second stage ideal activity plan then becomes subject of activity risk analysis.
iii) The third phase is to allocate resources. iv) The last step is scheduling. Advantages: i) Helps to identify relationship and monitor process. ii) Provides effective budget management.

Types of scheduling: 1) Project Management Scheduling 2) Operating System Scheduling 3) Production and Manufacturing scheduling. Sequencing and Scheduling. Sequencing the activities: Sequence activities are the process of identifying and documenting relationship among the project activities. The fundamental reason for sequence activities process is finalizing interrelationship of activities to finish the project scope and achieve the task objectives. Tools/Techniques: Precedence Diagramming Method PDM, Arrowing Diagramming Method ADM, Dependency Determination.

Scheduling the activities: The scheduling has had to take account of availability of staff and the way in which the activities have been allocated to them. Project Managers usually know the expected completion date of a project long before they plan and schedule the activities required to finish the project. Tools/Techniques: Gantt Charts, Critical Path Method CPM, Program Evaluation and Review Technique, Resource Leveling and smoothing, Milestones Charts. Sequencing means Order of activities. Scheduling means Timeline and allocation of activities.

Network Planning Model: Project activities and their interactions are modeled as networks in these project scheduling systems. Time moves from left to right in the network. Steps: Define the overall system, select module A B C D, check specifications, Design module A B C D, Module Code/Test, Integrate/Test System.

There are two best techniques: 1) CPM Critical Path Method 2) PERT Program Evaluation and Review Technique. Both use the Activity on Arrow approach to visualize the project as a network where activities are drawn as arrows joining circles or nodes which represent the possible start or completion on an activity or set of activities. Scheduling techniques model the projects activities and their relationships as a Network.

Activity Relationships FS SS SF FF: After determining the activities we analyze them to see if there is any relationship between them. There are 4 types of relationships between Action A and B: 1) Finish-to-Start FS 2) Finish-to-Finish FF 3) Start-to-Start SS 4) Start-to-Finish SF.

1) Finish-to-Start FS: A logical connection in which a successor venture cannot start until a predecessor activity has finished. It only affects the dependent activity B. 2) Start-to-Start SS: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot start until a predecessor activity has started. The start of activity A drives the start of activity B.

3) Finish-to-Finish FF: A logical relationship in which a successor activity cannot finish until a predecessor activity has finished. So if activity B is bound with this relation it means it cannot finish till the predecessor activity A is finished. 4) Start-to-Finish SF: A logical connection in which a successor activity cannot finish until a predecessor activity has started.

Forward Pass and Backward Pass technique. i) Forward Pass Technique: Forward pass is a technique to move forward through network diagram to determining project duration and finding the critical path or free float of the project. Used to calculate Earliest Start ES and Earliest Finish EF times of each activity. Formula: ES = max EF all predecessors, EF = ES + Duration.

ii) Backward Pass Technique: Backward pass represents moving backward to the end result to calculate late start or to find if there is any slack in the activity. Used to calculate Latest Start LS and Latest Finish LF times of each activity. Formula: LF = min LS of all successors, LS = LF - Duration. Helps determine the slack float and the critical path.

Critical Path and Remedies: The critical path in a project is the longest path of activities from start to finish where each activity has zero slack float. Any delay in these activities will delay the whole project. It is found using Forward Pass and Backward Pass techniques. Total Float = LS - ES or LF - EF. Free Float = Lowest ES of successor - EF.

Remedies for Critical Path Delays: 1) Crashing: Add extra resources to shorten activity duration. 2) Fast Tracking: Perform activities in parallel instead of sequential. 3) Reallocation of resources: Shift resources from non-critical tasks to critical tasks. 4) Scope Reduction: Reduce or simplify project requirements. 5) Improved Monitoring and control: Regularly track progress with tools like Gantt charts, CPM, PERT, or project Management software.
